subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear overall sequence (introduction â background â derivations â application â analysis â conclusion)
Purposeful organization with contents page, numbered headings and tables
Logical transitions between sections and a clear structure
weaknesses: Digressions (e.g., portfolio screenshots) interrupt the flow
Repeated definitions of basic terms add unnecessary length
Inconsistent sub-section numbering and misplacement of some material in the body
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (coherence and organization) meet band-3 criteria, showing good logical development and clear structure. The third strand (conciseness) falls in band 2 due to repeated explanations and unnecessary detail. Overall, a solid band 3 performance justifies a score of 3.

strengths: Use of multiple representations (formulas, tables, graphs, binomial-tree diagram)
Correct referencing of figures and tables in the text
Identification of key symbols (Sâ, X, Ï) and functions
weaknesses: Inconsistent and incorrect notation (Î´ vs Ï, ââ âTâ, placeholder symbols)
Omissions in definitions and mis-typed formulas (dâ expression, risk-free portfolio)
Graphs lack axis labels and some derivations contain leaps and typographical errors
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Only one strand attains band 3; the other two are at band 2. Mathematical language and clarity are only partially consistent, yielding an overall band 2 performance and a score of 2.

strengths: Personalized introduction linking the Wharton competition to the mathematical aim
Clear justification of model-choice decisions based on personal interests
Evidence of independent tweaks (MAE/RMSE comparison and volatility optimization)
weaknesses: Most procedures follow standard textbook methods with limited extension
Creativity and initiative are modest, with few novel ideas
Independent thinking is significant but not outstanding
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (independent thinking and personal approach) are in band 2; one strand (creativity) is at band 1. This distribution supports a band 2 overall score of 2.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on model suitability linked to initial aims
Thoughtful comparison of MAE/RMSE and discussion of discrepancies
Clear evaluation of outcomes with suggestions for future improvements
weaknesses: Reflection lacks depth in probing assumptions and methodological limitations
Critical analysis remains qualitative without integrating quantitative results fully
Evaluation is not comprehensiveâfuture implications are suggested but not rigorously tested
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands are placed in band 2, demonstrating meaningful but not substantial reflection, thoughtful analysis but not deep insight, and clear but limited evaluation. A band 2 score of 2 is appropriate.

strengths: Relevant use of Black-Scholes PDE, Itoâs lemma outline, binomial tree and error metrics
Mathematics is fully commensurate with the SL course level
Good manipulation of formulas to compute option prices
weaknesses: Minor notation errors (âV/âs vs âV/âS, mis-typed constants)
Computational slips (dâ value, risk-neutral probability misprint)
Inconsistent parameters (time-step T mismatches) and missing method outline for PDE solution
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands are solidly in band 4 (relevance and level appropriateness) and one strand (understanding and accuracy) is in band 3 due to minor errors. This profile matches an overall band 4, giving a score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: Arguments are mostly understandable, but derivations contain leaps (e.g., Ito step omits terms; risk-free portfolio expression misstated) and typographical slips obscure meaning.  Logical flow within lengthy lists is sometimes weak.  Consequently, communication shows âsomeâ clarity rather than the âgoodâ demanded for band 3.

The student shows sound understanding of formula inputs and can manipulate them to obtain option prices, but computational slips (e.g., mis-typed constants, inconsistent rounding) and a partially flawed derivation indicate minor errors.  Thus understanding is âgood with minor errorsââband 3.

The student includes formulas, tables of computed prices, scatter/line graphs, and a binomial-tree diagram.  These representations generally complement one another and are correctly referenced in the text, although axis labels are occasionally minimal.  Effectiveness outweighs minor issues, warranting band 3.

The report follows a clear sequence (introduction â background â derivations â application â analysis â conclusion) that can be tracked without difficulty.  Transitions between sections are generally logical, and arguments usually build on prior statements.  Nevertheless, a number of digressions (e.g., Wharton portfolio screenshots, lengthy definitions repeated later) interrupt the flow and occasionally force the reader to re-orient.  Overall coherence is good but not uniformly sustained, justifying a solid, not exemplary, band-3 placement.

Beyond the basic comparative aim and a simple optimisation tweak, the exploration offers limited novel ideas or innovative data collection.  The work remains largely standard, so only âsome creativity or initiativeâ is presentâband 1.

MAE and RMSE are calculated and compared, and reasons for discrepancies are sketched.  However, the stochastic-calculus derivation is not critiqued, and sources of estimation error (input accuracy, discretisation) are only briefly mentioned.  Thoughtful, but not âdeep and insightfulâ, matching band 2.

Outcomes of both models are weighed, and future improvements (stochastic volatility, Monte-Carlo) are suggested, showing a clear evaluative element.  Yet the discussion remains qualitative and does not test proposed changes, so comprehensiveness is lacking.  Band 2 is therefore appropriate.

The student comments on what was learned about model suitability and links conclusions to earlier aims, demonstrating reflection more than mere description.  Reflection appears at several points but seldom probes assumptions deeply, suggesting âmeaningfulâ rather than âsubstantialââband 2.

Key symbols ( Sâ, X, Ï ) and functions (N(Â·)) are identified, and some stochastic-calculus notation appears.  Still, several symbols are mis-typed (Î´ vs Ï, ââ âTâ), and formatting errors (â$\omega_{\rm max}$â) reduce precision.  Such inconsistency shows only âsome appropriateâ mathematical language, placing the strand in band 2.

The narrative is framed around the studentâs experience in the Wharton competition and career interests, and model-choice decisions are justified personally.  Such contextualisation shows clear evidence of a personal approach, albeit within a conventional topic, placing it in band 2.

Most paragraphs are relevant to pricing-model comparison, yet repeated explanations of basic option terminology and extended personal anecdotes add length without advancing mathematics.  Several bullet lists duplicate earlier text, and the same call-price calculation is re-introduced in two sections.  Hence the exploration is âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ, corresponding to band 2.

The mathematics (Black-Scholes PDE, Ito lemma outline, binomial tree, MAE/RMSE) is clearly relevant to option pricing and applied in context.  While coverage is not exhaustive, it is more than âappropriateâ and less than âcomprehensiveâ, aligning with band 4.

Choosing identical data sets for both models, performing MAE/RMSE, and attempting a volatility optimisation illustrate noticeable personal thinking beyond textbook replication.  However, most mathematical content follows well-known textbook procedures, and independent extensions remain limited.  This fits âsignificant evidenceâ (band 2), but not âoutstandingâ.

A contents page, numbered headings, tables, and separate conclusion demonstrate purposeful organisation.  Each main heading addresses one aspect of the aim, and the reader can locate material quickly.  However, sub-section numbering becomes inconsistent (e.g., â3.3.4â after â3.2.3â), and some material that belongs in appendices (statistical output) sits in the body.  These flaws indicate good but not outstanding structure, matching band 3.

Differential equations, logarithms, exponentials and probability concepts match HL expectations.  The derivation attempts stochastic calculus but lacks full rigor; nevertheless, level is fully commensurate with the course, deserving band 4 (not 5 or 6, which require extended mastery or original application).

annotations.comment: The introduction effectively personalizes the exploration by linking the studentâs Wharton competition experience to the mathematical aim, demonstrating clear evidence of a personal approach.
The formula $$C=S_0 N(d_1)-Xe^{-rT}N(d_2)$$ is introduced without immediate definitions of $$d_1$$ and $$d_2$$. Insert their expressions or reference to their section to improve logical flow.
In the Ito expansion step, the derivative term is written as $$\partial V/\partial s\,dt$$ instead of $$\partial V/\partial S\,dS$$. Correct notation to reflect the stochastic differential.
The risk-free portfolio expression is unclear: â$$-(V(S,t))$$â appears without context. Clarify or correct this equation to ensure precise mathematical communication.
List of input variables includes $$\omega_{\rm{eff}}$$ instead of the standard notation $$\sigma$$. Replace with commonly accepted symbols and explain any novel notation.
The background section repeats basic option definitions later in the text, adding unnecessary length. Consider referencing earlier definitions or relocating detailed basics to an appendix.
Several assumption list items use placeholder symbols (e.g., â\hat{\mathbf{\cdot}}â, â\divâ) instead of clear bullet notation. Standardize symbols for clarity in mathematical language.
Symbol inconsistency: the volatility term is alternately denoted by $$\delta$$ and $$\sigma$$. Standardize to $$\sigma$$ to maintain accuracy throughout the derivation.
The solution of the Black-Scholes PDE is stated without any outline of the method (e.g., change of variables). Provide a brief overview of the steps leading to the closed-form solution.
Calculated $$d_1$$ value (-0.0975) appears to conflict with the numeric substitution. Recompute $$d_1$$ carefully to ensure understanding and accuracy.
The graph lacks labeled axes and units, which reduces the effectiveness of the representation. Add clear axis titles (e.g., âStock Price ($)â) and scales.
The relation $$d_2=d_1-\partial\sqrt{T}$$ misuses the partial symbol. It should read $$d_2=d_1-\sigma\sqrt{T}$$ for consistency with the earlier definition.
Table 1 caption reads âCall for action priceâ; revise to âCall option priceâ to use standard terminology and improve presentation clarity.
The binomial up/down factors are introduced inline but without equation numbering. Present them as a numbered displayed equation for easier reference.
The risk-neutral probability formula duplicates the same expression for both q and (1âq). The second should read $$1-q=\frac{u-e^{r\Delta t}}{u-d}$$. Correct this misprint.
The section on computational complexity is narrative-heavy. A summary table or chart contrasting runtime complexity (e.g., O(n) vs O(nÂ²)) would strengthen presentation.
The correlation coefficient (âR-valueâ) is quoted without definition or context (e.g., R or RÂ²). Specify which metric is used and include its formula.
Ît is calculated with T=1 and n=3 correctly, but later risk-neutral probability uses T=0.5. Ensure consistency of time parameters throughout the binomial model application.
Table 2 lists stock prices at each step but omits the corresponding option values. Including both sets of data would strengthen the representation and clarity.
Table 3âs caption repeats the title in the table itself. A concise caption such as âOption prices in three-step binomial treeâ would improve conciseness.
The binomial expansion term uses an uppercase âXâ as a multiplication sign. Replace with a centered dot or explicit multiplication symbol to avoid confusion.
The strengths and weaknesses section is descriptive but lacks quantitative comparison. Suggest integrating MAE/RMSE results here to deepen critical analysis.
The intuitive explanation of Black-Scholes parameters uses real-world analogies effectively but should link back to the partial derivatives (the Greeks) for mathematical depth.
The comparative analysis introduces a table but defers discussion of error metrics until later. Consider previewing MAE/RMSE here to maintain coherence.
Table 4 comparing model and market prices omits an explicit error column. Adding absolute error would make the comparison more concise and informative.
